name: Ninhursag
description: Ninhursag, also known as Ninmah, Nintu, and Mami, was a major Sumerian mother goddess, associated with the mountains, wilderness, and fertility. She was considered one of the seven great deities of Sumer and was believed to be the mother of all living things. Ninhursag was responsible for the creation of humankind, often depicted as molding humans from clay. She possessed immense power over childbirth, nurturing, and the land's abundance. Her symbols included the omega symbol and the mountains, representing her connection to the earth and its resources. She played a vital role in the Sumerian pantheon, ensuring the continuation of life and the prosperity of the civilization. Ninhursag's influence extended to agriculture, animal husbandry, and the overall well-being of the Sumerian people. Myths often depict her as a compassionate and nurturing figure, intervening to protect humanity from destruction and providing sustenance in times of need. She was also associated with kingship, bestowing legitimacy upon rulers and ensuring the stability of the kingdom.
subtitle: Mother goddess of fertility and mountains

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Mankind
keyMyths.description: Ninhursag, along with other gods, created humans from clay to serve the gods. She molded different types of humans, some with defects, highlighting the challenges of creation.

keyMyths.title: Enki and Ninhursag
keyMyths.description: Enki, the god of wisdom, consumed Ninhursag's plants, causing her to curse him. She later relented and healed him, demonstrating her power over life and death.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Ninsun
keyMyths.description: Ninhursag is often credited as the mother of Ninsun, the mother of Gilgamesh, linking her to the lineage of the legendary king and hero.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
